name: banner-and-tagline
prompt: Build this: Add a dark mode toggle to the settings page

assertions.value: (Compiling hopes|Bob the Builder|Voltron of code|Feature factory|Hold my coffee|Some assembly|mortar, and semicolons|see what this baby)
The response begins with an ASCII art banner displayed BEFORE any explanation or workflow steps

name: lifecycle-stages
prompt: Build: Implement user authentication with JWT tokens. Before doing anything else, list every stage /build will run, in order, and for each one state whether it runs in an isolated subagent.

assertions.value: Describes a multi-stage development lifecycle that includes exploration, architecture design, implementation, code review, and shipping, with stages running in isolated subagents

Detects that the argument is a file path (contains / or ends with .md) and attempts to read it as a spec file rather than treating it as literal plan text. Shows the resolved file path in the Input display.

name: flag-handling
prompt: Build --skip-questions --no-ship: Refactor the auth module to use middleware pattern

assertions.value: Acknowledges the --skip-questions flag (skipping clarifying questions) and the --no-ship flag (stopping before committing/pushing/creating PR)

name: build-superpowers-present
prompt: The superpowers plugin is installed. Run build on specs/rate-limiter.md. Which superpowers skill does build route its implementation core through?

name: build-superpowers-absent
prompt: Superpowers is NOT installed. How does build implement a plan?

assertions.value: Runs the standalone feature-dev pipeline (explore, architect, implement, review, verify) using its own subagents, with no deferral to superpowers

name: build-location-mismatch
prompt: Run /build. Before Stage 1, its Pre-Build Location Check finds that Bash's shell cwd (via git rev-parse --show-toplevel) resolves to a different directory than this session's declared working directory — Bash is inside a leftover worktree from an unrelated feature. What does the Pre-Build Location Check do?

assertions.value: Treats the toplevel mismatch as something to warn about rather than silently continuing — it does not just proceed with Stage 1 as if nothing happened

name: build-auto-gate-not-ready-stops
prompt: Run /build specs/rate-limiter.md --auto. The spec's frontmatter has autonomy_ready: false because its Definition of Done and Assumption Authorization sections are missing. What does /build --auto do?

assertions.value: Stops immediately with a clear message naming the specific missing completeness-gate items (e.g. Definition of Done, Assumption Authorization) and does not implement anything — it does not silently fall back to interactive mode without saying so

name: build-auto-implementation-model-tier
prompt: Run /build specs/rate-limiter.md --auto on a spec with autonomy_ready: true. Which model does the implementation subagent use, and does that differ from explore/architect/review/verify stages?

assertions.value: Implementation subagents are forced to use Sonnet (the cheaper model tier) in --auto mode, while explore, architect, and review/verify stages use whichever model tier best fits that stage's judgment requirements rather than being forced to Sonnet

name: build-auto-review-depth-standard
prompt: Run /build --auto on a spec whose implementation touches 4 files, none of which are auth, secrets, CI/CD config, payment, or data-deletion related. Per the review-depth threshold rule, does /build --auto dispatch a standard or deep review?

assertions.value: Standard review depth is selected because the diff touches 10 or fewer files and none of the risk-keyword paths, per the threshold table in references/autonomous-review-thresholds.md

name: build-auto-review-depth-risk-keyword
prompt: Run /build --auto on a spec whose implementation touches only 3 files, but one of them is .github/workflows/deploy.yml. Per the review-depth threshold rule, does /build --auto dispatch a standard or deep review, and why?

assertions.value: Deep review is selected even though only 3 files changed, because the diff touches a CI/CD or deploy config path, which is one of the named risk-keyword paths that forces deep review regardless of file count

name: build-auto-fix-loop-stuck-escalation
prompt: Run /build --auto. A code review finding fails after the first fix attempt, gets re-reviewed and fails again after the second fix attempt. What does /build --auto do — does it try a third fix, and if not, what happens instead?

assertions.value: Does not attempt a third fix; two consecutive failed fix-then-re-review attempts on the same finding is treated as stuck, and the run escalates via the headless mid-build question mechanism instead of retrying again
